A roundup of what I've been reading over the colder months.

Winter is my favorite season for reading. The shorter days and cold weather make it easy to justify long stretches with a book and a cup of tea.

## The highlights

**"Seeing Like a State" by James C. Scott** — A fascinating look at how large institutions try to make the world legible, and how those efforts often backfire. Changed how I think about top-down planning.

**"Several Short Sentences About Writing" by Verlyn Klinkenborg** — Not a writing manual so much as a way of seeing. Every sentence in this book earns its place. I've been re-reading sections before I sit down to write.

**"The Mushroom at the End of the World" by Anna Lowenhaupt Tsing** — About matsutake mushrooms, global supply chains, and what it means to live in the ruins of capitalism. Weird and wonderful.

## What I noticed

This winter I gravitated toward books that challenge how I see systems — political, ecological, linguistic. I think there's a thread connecting all of these: they're all about the gap between how things are supposed to work and how they actually work.

## Up next

I have a growing stack that includes some fiction I've been putting off. Spring feels like the right time for novels.

Why sharing your process matters more than sharing your results.

There's something powerful about letting people watch you work. Not the polished, curated version — the real thing, with all its false starts and dead ends.

## Why build in public?

Building in public isn't about performance. It's about accountability and connection. When you share your process, you invite others into your thinking. You create surface area for serendipity.

I've found that the act of explaining what I'm doing forces me to understand it better. Writing about a problem often reveals the solution. And the feedback loop from sharing — even to a small audience — keeps me moving forward on days when motivation is low.

## The uncomfortable parts

Let's be honest: it's not always comfortable. There's a vulnerability in showing work that isn't finished. You worry about being judged for the messy middle, for the ideas that don't pan out.

But I've learned that most people aren't judging. They're relating. They're seeing their own struggles reflected back. And that's where the real value lives — not in the finished product, but in the shared experience of making something.

## What I'm trying

Right now, I'm experimenting with writing more frequently about smaller things. Not every post needs to be a manifesto. Sometimes a few paragraphs about something I noticed or learned is enough.

The goal isn't to build an audience. It's to build a practice.

Reflections on what it means to care deeply about how things are made.

I've been thinking a lot about craft lately — what it means to care about how something is made, not just what it does.

## The difference between good and great

Good work solves the problem. Great work solves the problem in a way that feels inevitable, like it couldn't have been done any other way. That feeling of inevitability is the hallmark of craft.

It shows up in software when the API feels natural. In writing when a sentence lands perfectly. In cooking when the flavors are balanced without any single one dominating. You can't always articulate what makes it work, but you can feel it.

## Curiosity as fuel

I think craft is sustained by curiosity. The craftsperson asks "what if?" and "why?" long after everyone else has moved on. They notice the details that others skip. They find the extra 10% that transforms something from functional to delightful.

Curiosity keeps you from settling. It makes you tear apart something that's working fine because you sense it could be better. That's uncomfortable, but it's where the growth happens.

## The practice

Craft isn't a destination. It's a daily practice of paying attention — to your tools, to your materials, to the people who will use what you make. Some days the work flows. Other days it's a grind. But showing up either way is the whole game.

On the meta-skill of figuring out how to pick up new things effectively.

The most useful skill I've developed isn't any specific technology or technique. It's learning how to learn.

## The myth of talent

We love stories about naturals — people who just "get it" from the start. But the more I talk to people who are excellent at what they do, the more I realize they all have one thing in common: they've gotten very good at the process of learning itself.

They know when to read and when to practice. They know how to break down a complex skill into smaller pieces. They know when they're stuck and how to get unstuck.

## What works for me

**Start with the whole, then zoom in.** I like to get a rough sense of the entire landscape before diving into details. This gives me a mental map that helps me place new information as I encounter it.

**Teach what you learn.** Explaining something to someone else is the fastest way to find the gaps in your understanding. If I can't explain it simply, I don't understand it well enough.

**Embrace confusion.** The uncomfortable feeling of not understanding is a signal that learning is happening. I've trained myself to lean into that feeling instead of running from it.

## The long game

Learning compounds. The more you know, the easier it is to learn the next thing, because you have more mental models to connect new information to. The early stages are the hardest. But if you stick with the process, it gets easier — and more rewarding — over time.

Looking back on a year of changes, projects, and lessons learned.

Another year in the books. 2025 was a year of transition — some things ended, new things began, and I learned more than I expected along the way.

## What went well

**Writing consistently.** I published more this year than any previous year. Not all of it was great, but the volume helped me find my voice. The best pieces came from topics I was genuinely wrestling with, not topics I thought would perform well.

**Saying no more often.** I turned down projects that didn't excite me. This was hard at first — it felt like leaving opportunities on the table. But the space it created let me go deeper on the things that mattered.

**Learning new tools.** I picked up a few new technologies this year that changed how I work. The initial investment felt slow, but the compounding returns have been worth it.

## What I'd do differently

I spent too long on a couple of projects that I should have abandoned earlier. The sunk cost fallacy is real, and I'm still learning to recognize when persistence becomes stubbornness.

I also didn't take enough breaks. Sustainable pace is something I understand intellectually but struggle with in practice.

## Looking ahead

For 2026, I want to focus on depth over breadth. Fewer projects, more investment in each one. More time reading and thinking, less time reacting. Let's see how it goes.
